perturbation.original_text: 1.2 Term This agreement is for a term of ten (10) years. 1.3 Renewal This agreement will be automatically renewed at the end of every ten (10) year term, with each subsequent term of renewal being for a ten (10) year term. A six (6) months notice must be given by either party of their intention to terminate relations due to any reason other than breach of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 Term This agreement is for a term of ten (10) years. 1.3 Renewal This agreement will be automatically renewed unless either party objects within a reasonable time frame. A six (6) months notice must be given by either party of their intention to terminate relations due to any reason other than breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies automatic renewal for ten-year terms. The modified clause removes the specific ten-year term for renewal, introducing ambiguity by using 'reasonable timeframe'. This contradicts the initial term stated in section 1.2, creating uncertainty about the renewal duration. Section 13.2 refers to a ten (10) year term renewal, and this change introduces an in-text contradiction since the agreement could now be interpreted as automatically renewing for indefinite periods shorter or longer than the original duration, directly impacting section 13.2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.2 The Manufacturer grants exclusive rights to the Customer for the term of ten (10) years from the date of the signing of this agreement and for an indefinite period upon the customer fulfilling the minimum annual purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original agreement specifies a ten-year renewal term. The modified agreement introduces the term 'reasonable timeframe,' creating ambiguity. This directly contradicts section 13.2, which grants exclusive rights to the Customer for a 'term of ten (10) years' and an 'indefinite period.' The 'reasonable timeframe' could be interpreted as shorter or longer than ten years, conflicting with the specified ten-year term and the indefinite period based on purchase requirements. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product, provided that the Customer shall within seven (7) days after receipt of such alleged defective Product, notify the Manufacturer of its rejection and either: (i) request to destroy in field for credit of the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs (with approval), or (ii) request a Return Material Authorization ("RMA") number and within seven (7) days of receipt of the RMA number from the Manufacturer return such rejected Product to the Manufacturer. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer shall credit to the Customer the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product, provided that the Customer shall notify the Manufacturer of its rejection and either: (i) request to destroy in field for credit of the value of the defective product (with approval), or (ii) request a Return Material Authorization ("RMA") number from the Manufacturer return such rejected Product to the Manufacturer. Products not rejected shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer shall credit to the Customer the value of the defective product.
perturbation.explanation: The original section 5.3 specifies a seven-day timeframe for both notifying the manufacturer of rejection and returning the product after receiving the RMA. This has been removed creating ambiguity and an in-text contradiction. Section 5.3 originally outlines the conditions and processes needed to be deemed rejected, without the 'seven (7) days' timeframe and 'associated shipping costs', the contract now lacks clarity, leading to confusion and potential disagreements between the parties. This change contradicts earlier mentions of specific timelines, undermining the overall clarity in sections 5.1 and 5.4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 Shipping Products delivered pursuant to the terms of this Agreement shall be suitably packed for shipment in the Manufacturer's standard shipping cartons, marked for shipment to the destination specified in the Customer's Purchase Order, and delivered to the destination Ex Works. The Customer agrees to pay freight, insurance and any associated expenses. The Customer agrees to help the Manufacturer select the most appropriate carrier for each of the Territories. All freight, insurance, and other shipping expenses shall be paid by the Customer.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'seven (7) days' and 'associated shipping costs' in the changed Section 5.3 directly contradicts the implied timeframe for rejection and return of defective products and cost responsibilities, creating ambiguity. The original text defines the specific timeframe for rejection and product return, whereas the changed text removes this, conflicting with the Customer's obligation to pay 'freight, insurance and any associated expenses' as outlined in Section 5.1. The customer has no timeframe for returning the products to get a refund, causing contradiction and conflicts. Thus, these omissions introduce a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13.1 The Manufacturer hereby appoints the Customer to be the sole and exclusive agent for the promotion, sales, marketing, distribution and administration of the products listed in schedule A of this agreement based on minimum annual product purchase requirements as listed in Schedule B of this agreement. 13.2 The Manufacturer grants exclusive rights to the Customer for the term of ten (10) years from the date of the signing of this agreement and for an indefinite period upon the customer fulfilling the minimum annual purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 The Manufacturer hereby appoints the Customer to be the agent for the products listed in schedule A of this agreement. 13.2 The Manufacturer grants rights to the Customer for the term of years from the date of the signing of this agreement and for an indefinite period upon the customer fulfilling the purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original section 13.1 gives the customer sole and exclusive rights. This has been removed creating an in-text contradiction. Additionally, in 13.2 'ten (10)' and 'minimum annual' have also been removed respectively. With no agent status, and the term and purchase requirements left ambiguous, it makes the purpose of the agreement contradictory to that of section 3.6 which states that: 'The Manufacturer covenants not to sell any product listed in this agreement, or product name (as listed in schedule A of this agreement) to any other party without prior written consent of the Customer'. With the exclusive agent removed, as well as the term 'ten (10)', and 'minimum annual' there's no real reason to have section 3.6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6 Sale of Product The Manufacturer covenants not to sell any product listed in this agreement, or product name (as listed in schedule A of this agreement) to any other party without prior written consent of the Customer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'sole and exclusive' agent status granted to the customer in the original text of section 13.1. This directly contradicts section 3.6, which states that the Manufacturer cannot sell the product to any other party without the Customer's consent. Without the 'sole and exclusive' status, there's no clear justification for the manufacturer being restricted by section 3.6, as the customer is no longer necessarily the sole distributor. The omissions of 'ten (10)' and 'minimum annual' also create ambiguity around the term and purchase requirements, further weakening the relationship and therefore the justification for section 3.6, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
